sit: You're in tight to the net — five feet out. The goalie is set. You have the puck on your backhand but there's barely any time. What do you do?
opts: Try to switch the puck from your backhand to your forehand for a stronger shot
Release your backhand immediately — the goalie is set for the forehand anyway
Pass to a teammate outside the slot
Skate around the net to get a better shooting angle

sit: Your team is cycling in the offensive zone. The cycle isn't working — the defenders have perfectly matched your movement for 20 seconds and nothing is opening. What should you do?
opts: Keep cycling the same way — something has to open up for a shot eventually
Stop the cycle — dump the puck deep, change lines, and come back with a new entry
Shoot from the corner area
Pass all the way back to the blue line point

sit: Your team just got called for a penalty. Before you leave the ice, what should the remaining four players know?
opts: Nothing really — just go out and defend hard
Their specific penalty kill assignments — who covers the point, who covers the half-wall, and what the plan is if they get the puck
To skate as fast as they can and chase every puck carrier around the zone without ever stopping until the penalty ends
To surround the puck carrier at all times with all four skaters so there's no chance for a clean pass or shot on the goalie

sit: You're the last defender back and two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. Your goalie is set. One attacker has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
opts: Commit hard to the puck carrier on the left and try to take the puck off their stick before they can make any kind of play
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter all the way to the right post to make sure they can't tap in any pass that comes across to the back door
Back up all the way to your own crease so you're right next to the goalie and can cover any rebound that comes out on a shot

sit: Your team is winning by one goal with two minutes left. You win a faceoff in the offensive zone. What's most important?
opts: Take a big slap shot immediately on the faceoff draw to try to extend your lead early
Control the puck, cycle below the dots, use every second — only shoot on a high-quality chance
Dump the puck deep into the far corner and chase it hard with all your forwards
Pass the puck all the way back to your own zone to be safe and kill more clock

sit: You're the D and you pick up the puck in your own corner. The forechecker is coming from your left. Your left winger is up the wall on your left — open, but the forechecker is between you and that pass. Your right winger is also up the wall on the right, completely open. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass to the left winger — they're your first option immediately
Pass to the right winger — the forechecker has already cut off the left
Carry it out yourself
Rim it around the boards

sit: You have the puck at the offensive blue line. A defender is closing hard from your right. Your center is open five feet behind you. Your only shooting lane is gone. What do you do?
opts: Try to hold the puck and fight through the pressure until a lane opens
Drop a back pass to your center — they have time and space and you don't
Shoot it right into the defender
Dump the puck deep into the corner and chase

sit: You're about to shoot from the slot. Before you release, you have a split second to look at the goalie. They're leaning hard to your right. Where should you shoot?
opts: Top right — shoot high over their shoulder on the same side they are leaning hard toward
Low left — the opposite side of where they're leaning. They can't recover in time.
Right at their chest — power wins
Wait for the goalie to reset their position before you decide to release the shot

sit: You're on a line change. Your linemates are still on the ice finishing a rush. The play is still alive. When do you go on?
opts: Go immediately — you're fresh and your team needs the help
Wait until the puck is dead, there's a stoppage, or your teammates are safely at the bench and can't be caught short
Jump onto the ice as soon as your teammate steps off, even if they're still in the play and skating hard
Go as soon as you feel ready and rested up

sit: Your team just won a puck battle in your own zone. Two of your forwards are already at center ice. The other team has one player back. A 3-on-1 is developing. What's the trigger?
opts: Wait until all three forwards are together in the neutral zone first
Go immediately — the 3-on-1 window closes fast as the other team gets back
Regroup back at your own blue line first
Dump it in deep and head to the bench

sit: You're a defenseman in your own zone. The opposing winger has the puck in the corner and keeps looking at the player in the slot. What do you do?
opts: Watch the puck carrier closely and wait for the pass to actually happen first
Step in front of the anticipated pass lane — intercept it before it arrives
Skate into the corner to pressure the puck carrier
Back up and hug your crease

sit: You're defending in your own zone. An attacker cuts from the left side to the right side in front of the net. Your partner was covering them but now they're on your side. What do you do?
opts: Ignore the attacker completely and trust that your partner will just follow them over
Call switch and take the attacker coming to your side so your partner can recover
Call for the referee to whistle a penalty on the crossing play
Back up all the way to the crease and let the attacker set up wherever they want to go

sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. All three forwards are on the left side of the ice — bunched up. The entire right side is empty. You're the right winger. What do you do?
opts: Stay on the left side of the ice where the puck is and help your teammates battle for possession along the boards in the zone
Move immediately to the right side — the space is there and the D will have to follow you or leave you open
Go to the bench for a line change
Stand in front of the opposing net

sit: Your team is on the penalty kill and in a box formation. The power play moves the puck quickly around the outside. One of your PK players starts chasing every pass. What goes wrong?
opts: Nothing at all — chasing pucks is actually a great PK strategy
Chasing breaks the box — gaps open up and the power play gets into the dangerous areas you were protecting
Chasing tires out the power play faster
Chasing makes the power play players hesitate and second-guess every pass they make around the outside of the box setup

sit: You're the center on a rush. The defenders are both backing up. You're at the top of the circles moving at full speed. You have a shooting lane. What do you do?
opts: Pass to a winger immediately — passing first on a rush is always the right play
Shoot — you're at full speed and the goalie has to set against a moving shooter
Pull up and stickhandle
Dump the puck in deep and reset

sit: Your D picks up the puck. The forechecker is coming from the left. Your left winger is covered. Your right winger is also covered. Your center is late getting back. What's the option?
opts: Force a hard pass to one of the covered wingers and hope they can win the battle down the wall
Reverse behind the net — the forechecker on the left has to follow, which buys time for the center to get back
Carry the puck straight out yourself and try to skate through the forechecker coming from the left side
Shoot the puck down the ice and take the icing whistle to get a fresh faceoff in your own zone

sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. A power play player is standing right in front of your goalie screening them. The puck is at the point. What's your immediate priority?
opts: Go pressure the defenseman at the point who has the puck right now so they can't get off a clean shot through the screen
Move the screener out of the crease — your goalie can't see the shot and that's more dangerous than the shot itself
Stay in a passing lane so nothing gets through
Back up all the way to the crease yourself to help the goalie block any shot that might be coming from the point

sit: Your team wins a faceoff in the offensive zone. The opposing center had to tie the draw. The puck goes back to your defenseman at the point. Before the opposing center gets their bearings, what should you do?
opts: Wait patiently for the play to develop normally before you decide what to do with your positioning in the offensive zone right now
Drive hard to the net immediately — the opposing center just spent all their energy on the draw and the lane to the net may be briefly open
Skate all the way over to the corner and see what kind of play develops down low before you commit to any real position
Call for a pass from the defenseman at the point so you can get the puck back and try to set up a play in the high slot

sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. Two teammates are available — one is at the half-wall with a clear shot lane, and one is right in front of the net with no lane but good positioning. Which do you pass to?
opts: The teammate in front of the net — they're in the best scoring position right on the doorstep
The one at the half-wall — they have the open lane right now and can shoot immediately
Hold the puck yourself
Pass back to the point to reset the play

sit: Your team is in the defensive zone. The puck goes from the corner to the half-wall. As the nearest forward, you move to pressure the half-wall player. Who covers your corner assignment?
opts: Nobody — you handle both
Your D partner rotates to cover the corner behind you the rotation
Your winger on the other side skates across and trust the play to develop
You stay in both spots somehow right away

sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure in your own zone. Your D has the puck and is looking to pass. You're a winger and you're currently covered. When should you break to the wall to get the pass?
opts: Stay right where you are and trust that your defenseman will figure out the play on their own
Skate to the wall hard, using your speed to lose the forechecker for a split second — make the D's decision easy
Don't break to the wall until the forechecker has already left your area and given up the chase
Stand completely still in your spot and call loudly for the puck to get your D's attention

sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. You can shoot from where you are — a decent but not great angle. OR you can pass to a teammate in the slot who's slightly covered but would have a much better shot. What do you do?
opts: Shoot — you have the puck and a decent angle, so take the shot before anything changes
Pass to the slot player — the higher danger location is worth the slight coverage risk
Hold the puck and wait for a better chance
Pass all the way back to the point and let the defenseman set up a new play from there

sit: There's a loose puck in your defensive zone right in front of your net. You and an opposing player are racing to it at the same time. What's your priority?
opts: Get to the puck first and try to skate it all the way out of the zone yourself before the opponent can close on you
Get your body between the opponent and the puck — inside position wins this battle even if you get there second
Poke at the puck with your stick from a distance and try to knock it loose without getting into the physical battle at all
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and get the puck while you block the opposing player from reaching the front of the net

sit: Your team is on the power play trying to enter the offensive zone. The penalty killers are set at their blue line — two of them. How should your team attack?
opts: Carry the puck straight up the middle every time since you have the extra man advantage anyway
Use the extra man advantage: hold the puck until one PK player commits, then exploit what they've given up
Dump the puck deep into the corner and send all five skaters in hard to chase and recover it
Skate back into your own zone and regroup over and over until the PK gets tired and breaks

sit: The puck carrier has the puck in the offensive zone but is being smothered by defenders. You don't have the puck and a defender is near you. What's the best thing you can do?
opts: Stand still and call for the puck loudly so the puck carrier knows exactly where you are and can make the pass to you
Skate into open space — cut to the net, move to a seam. Make yourself available AND drag your defender out of the puck carrier's area.
Go help the puck carrier in the battle for the puck by skating right into the pile to try to get the puck loose for the team
Skate out to the blue line right away so you're ready to make a breakout pass if the other team gets the puck back and clears

sit: You're in the offensive zone and the puck is about to turn over. Before it does, where should you already be moving?
opts: Stay exactly where you are in the offensive zone and wait to see if the turnover really happens
Start transitioning early — turn toward your own zone before the turnover is complete. Get a head start.
Skate to the bench
Go to the corner

sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. A teammate is in the slot but a defender is between you and them. You can see a sliver of the teammate. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass through the defender — your teammate is in the slot and you can see them
Find a better option — a covered pass through a defender is high-risk. Look for the winger or the point.
Hold the puck and skate around
Take the shot from where you

sit: You're the defenseman at the offensive blue line. Your winger just gained possession in the corner. Should you pinch to join the attack below the dots?
opts: Yes — always pinch hard and join every attack so you can keep the puck deep in the zone
Only if the other winger or center is positioned to cover the blue line behind you — otherwise hold the line
Yes — pinch every single time your winger touches the puck, no matter who is covering behind you
No — never pinch from the blue line under any circumstances because it is always too risky
